9: kai gar ego anthropos eimi hypo exousian — for I also am a man under authority — the centurion's argument is a fortiori: if he can command soldiers at a distance because he himself is under Roman military authority (a chain of command that gives his words power), how much more can Jesus command disease at a distance, being himself under the ultimate authority. exousia — authority, the right and power to act — appears 10 times in Matthew, always associated with Jesus's teaching, healing, and forgiving (7:29, 9:6, 11:27, 28:18).
17: autos tas astheneias hemon elaben kai tas nosous ebastasen — he himself took our infirmities and bore our diseases — Matthew's citation of Isa 53:4 applies the Servant's vicarious bearing not to the cross but to the healing ministry. Labo (took) and bastazo (bore, carried) are the verbs of the Servant's substitutionary work. The healing miracles are not merely humanitarian acts but are themselves the Servant's carrying of human sickness — an extension of the same substitutionary principle that reaches its fullness in the passion.
1: katabanti de autō apo tou orous — when he came down from the mountain — katabanti dative aorist participle of katabainō: the descent from the mount of teaching (ch5–7) is the frame for the sequence of miracles in ch8–9 that demonstrate the same authority in deeds. Ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi — large crowds followed him — akoloutheo (to follow) is Matthew's discipleship verb; the crowds follow physically; the question is whether they follow in commitment.
2: lepros — a man with leprosy — lepros covers a range of skin conditions, not necessarily what modern medicine identifies as Hansen's disease; the significance is the Levitical uncleanness and social exclusion it imposed (Lev 13-14). Prosekynei autō — was bowing before him/worshipping him — proskyneō (to prostrate oneself, do obeisance): the gesture of approach to royalty or deity. Kyrie ean thelēs dynastai me katharisai — Lord, if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me — a careful formulation: the leper affirms Jesus's power without presuming his will.
3: kai ekteinas tēn cheira ēpsato autou — and stretching out his hand he touched him — ekteinas aorist participle: deliberately extending the hand toward the unclean; hēpsato aorist of haptō, to touch, to grasp. Under Levitical law, contact with a leper conveyed uncleanness to the one who touched; Jesus reverses the direction: his touch cleanses rather than contracting impurity. Thelō katharisthēti — I am willing; be cleansed — thelō (I will/want) answers the leper's conditional; katharisthi is an aorist passive imperative — Jesus does not ask the man to become clean but pronounces and effects his cleansing with a single word.
4: hora mēdeni eipēs — see that you tell no one — hora (see to it, take heed) + mēdeni eipēs (negative aorist subjunctive: tell no one). This is the first instance of the "messianic secrecy" motif in Matthew: Jesus limits publicity about his identity and works. Alla hypage seauton deixon tō hierei — but go, show yourself to the priest — deixon aorist imperative: the showing is to be the first act, before speaking to anyone. Kai prosenegke to dōron ho prosetaxen Mōysēs — and offer the gift Moses commanded — referring to the cleansing ritual of Lev 14:2-32. Eis martyrion autois — as a testimony to them: the restored leper becomes living proof before the religious authorities.
5: hekatontarchos — centurion (commander of 100 soldiers in the Roman army) — a Gentile military officer; the encounter follows immediately after the Jew with leprosy, setting up a Jew/Gentile contrast in responses to Jesus. Parakalōn auton — appealing to him, imploring him (parakaleō, to call alongside, to exhort or beseech): the centurion approaches not as a superior but as a supplicant.
6: ho pais mou — my servant/boy (pais) — a word covering both servant and child; it has more personal warmth than the standard slave-word doulos. The centurion's concern for his pais establishes the personal stakes. Beblētai en tē oikia paralytikos deinōs basanizomenos — has been thrown (= is lying paralyzed) at home, suffering terribly — beblētai perfect passive: currently in the state of having been cast down; deinōs basanizomenos present passive: continuously being tormented.
7: kagō elthōn therapeusō auton — I will come and heal him — the Greek can be read as a direct statement (I will come and heal him) or as a question with implied reluctance (Shall I come and heal him?, raising the social irregularity of a Jewish teacher entering a Gentile household). The ambiguity is unresolved in the text; what matters is that the centurion's response in v.8 presupposes the greater point: Jesus's authority does not require presence. Elthōn is an aorist participle of erchomai: having come.
8: kyrie ouk eimi hikanos — Lord, I am not worthy (hikanos, sufficient, fit, adequate) — the centurion uses the language of social hierarchy: he is not of the rank that would make his household appropriate for Jesus to enter. Alla monon eipe logō — but only say the word — monon (only, merely) + logō (dative: with/by a word alone): a single word from Jesus is sufficient because authority does not require proximity. Kai iathēsetai ho pais mou — and my servant will be healed — iathēsetai future passive: the healing is certain, conditioned only on Jesus's word.
10: ethaumasen — he marveled (thaumazō, to be astonished) — one of only two occasions in the Gospels where Jesus is said to marvel (the other is Mark 6:6, at unbelief); here he marvels at unusual faith. Para oudeni tosouten pistin en tō Israēl heuron — not in anyone in Israel have I found such faith — tosouten (so great, so large): the faith is not just faith but faith of exceptional magnitude. The observation establishes the inversion: Gentile faith exceeds the faith of the covenant people.
11: polloi apo anatolōn kai dysmōn hēxousin kai anaklitheēsontai — many from east and west will come and will recline at table — apo anatolōn kai dysmōn (from risings and settings of the sun, i.e., east and west): a comprehensive geographical merism. Anaklitheēsontai future passive of anaklinō (to recline at table): the eschatological banquet posture. Meta Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb en tē basileia tōn ouranōn — with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven — the patriarchs serve as the expected guests; the surprise is who else joins them.
12: hoi de huioi tēs basileias — the sons of the kingdom — those who by birth and covenant membership expected to inherit the kingdom; the phrase simultaneously acknowledges their special status and subverts the presumption that status guarantees entry. Ekblēthēsontai eis to skotos to exōteron — will be thrown into the outer darkness — ekballō future passive: the same verb used for exorcism (throwing out demons). Skotos to exōteron (the outermost darkness): outside the light of the feast. Ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn — there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth — a Matthean formula (also 13:42,50; 22:13; 24:51; 25:30): the reaction of those excluded from the eschatological celebration.
13: hōs episteusan — as you believed — hōs (as) + aorist subjunctive: the healing corresponds exactly and immediately to the faith expressed. Iathē ho pais — the servant was healed — aorist passive: completed action. En tē hōra ekeinē — at that very hour (hōra, hour; ekeinē, that specific one) — the temporal precision underlines that Jesus's word effects instantaneous healing at a distance, confirming the centurion's thesis in v.8.
14: tēn pentheran autou beblēmenēn kai pyresousan — his mother-in-law lying (in bed) and burning with fever — beblēmenēn perfect passive participle: she has been cast down, prostrated by illness; pyresousan present active participle: actively burning with fever (from pyressō, to have a fever, from pyr, fire). The two participles together describe both her posture (lying immobilized) and her condition (fever raging). The brief scene introduces Peter, whose household is now in Capernaum.
15: ēpsato tēs cheiros autēs kai aphēken autēn ho pyretos — he touched her hand and the fever left her — hēpsato aorist of haptō (touched): the same touch-verb as in 8:3 with the leper. Aphēken autēn ho pyretos — the fever left her (aphiēmi aorist: to leave, release) — a near-personification: the fever is spoken of as an agent that departs. Kai ēgerthē kai diēkonei autō — and she rose and was serving him — ēgerthē aorist passive (she was raised); diēkonei imperfect active: the ongoing service. The imperfect marks the restoration as complete and immediately productive.
16: daimonizomonous pollous prosēnenkan autō kai exebalen ta pneumata logō — many demon-possessed were brought to him and he drove out the spirits with a word — logō (dative: by/with a word alone): the same principle as the centurion's faith (v.8); Jesus's authority operates through speech. Kai pantas tous kakōs echontas etherapeusen — and he healed all who were ill (kakōs echō, to have badly, to be in a bad condition: a Greek idiom for illness or suffering). The summary verse covers both exorcism and healing, preparing for the formula-citation of v.17.
18: idōn de ho Iēsous ochlon peri auton ekeleusen apelthein eis to peran — seeing the crowd around him he commanded to depart to the other side — ekeleusen aorist of keleuō (to command, order), a strong directive. Eis to peran — to the other side (of the Sea of Galilee): a geographical transition that also carries narrative weight in Matthew as entry into Gentile territory.
19: kai proselthōn heis grammateus eipen autō — and one scribe approaching said to him — a scribe (grammateus), a professional Torah scholar and copyist, approaches as a potential disciple. Didaskale akolouthēsō soi hopou ean aperchē — Teacher (didaskalos, note: not "Lord" as Peter and the centurion use), I will follow you wherever you go — the promise is unqualified but the address is professional, not personal. Jesus's response addresses the unstated assumption behind the offer.
20: ho huios tou anthrōpou ouk echei pou tēn kephalēn klinē — the Son of Man has nowhere to lay (klinē aorist active subjunctive of klinō, to recline, lean) his head — ho huios tou anthrōpou is Jesus's self-designation drawn from Dan 7:13-14, where the figure receives eternal kingdom; Matthew's irony is that this cosmic figure has no fixed residence. Hai alōpekes phōleous echousin kai ta peteina tou ouranou kataskēnōseis — foxes have dens and birds of the sky have nests: even animals have fixed abode; the Son of Man does not. The saying tests whether the scribe's unqualified offer holds when the conditions are disclosed.
21: heteros de tōn mathētōn autou — another of his disciples — this person is already a disciple (mathētōn genitive), unlike the scribe of v.19. Kyrie epitrepsōn moi prōton apelthein kai thapsai ton patera mou — Lord, permit me first to go and bury my father — epitrepsōn aorist participle of permission: grant me permission. Prōton (first) is the key word; the request implies that family burial duty takes precedence over immediate discipleship response. In Jewish culture, burial of a parent was among the highest filial obligations.
22: aphes tous nekrous thapsai tous heautōn nekrous — let the dead bury their own dead — the most radical of Jesus's discipleship sayings. Two senses of nekrous are in play: the physically dead (those who can attend to the burial) and the spiritually dead (those who have no relation to the kingdom of life). Sy de akolouthei moi — but you, follow me — sy emphatic: you, in particular; akolouthei present imperative: begin following and continue. The saying does not forbid burial but places the urgency of kingdom-following above even the most pressing legitimate family obligation.
23: kai embanti autō eis to ploion ēkolouthēsan autō hoi mathētai autou — and when he got into the boat his disciples followed him — embanti dative aorist participle: the disciples' following is triggered by and coordinates with Jesus's boarding. The boat becomes the context for the test of faith in v.24-27. Akoloutheo here is both narrative and theological: physical following into the boat is the literal form of the discipleship Jesus demanded in v.22.
24: kai idou seismos megas egeneto en tē thalassē — and behold, a great shaking arose on the sea — seismos is the standard word for earthquake (seio, to shake); its application to a sea-storm is unusual and intensifying: the shaking is of the order of an earthquake-like upheaval. Hōste to ploion kalyptesthai hypo tōn kymatōn — so that the boat was being covered by the waves — present passive: the covering is ongoing and the situation worsening. Autos de ekatheuden — but he was sleeping — ekatheuden imperfect: he continued sleeping; the contrast between the disciples' terror and Jesus's sleep underlines the nature of the test.
25: kai proselthontes ēgeiran auton legontes kyrie sōson apollymetha — and approaching they woke him saying: Lord, save us, we are perishing — sōson aorist imperative of sōzō (to save, rescue): the same verb used for eternal salvation; here it is physical rescue but the semantic range is never absent in Matthew. Apollymetha present: we are in the process of perishing right now. The address kyrie (Lord) marks the disciples as distinct from the scribe of v.19 who used didaskalos (Teacher).
26: ti deiloi este oligopistoi — why are you cowardly (deilos, fearful, cowardly — a word of character failure, not merely an emotion), you of little faith (oligopistoi, the compound from 6:30) — Jesus addresses the character of the fear before addressing the storm. Tote egertheis epetimēsen tois anemois kai tē thalassē — then rising he rebuked the winds and the sea — epetimēsen is the word used for rebuking demons (8:16; 17:18): the storm is addressed as a hostile power subject to command. Kai egeneto galēnē megalē — and there was a great calm (galēnē, the specific word for windless, flat-calm sea).
27: hoi de anthrōpoi ethaumasan legontes potapos estin houtos — the men marveled saying: what kind of person is this — potapos (what sort of, of what kind) asks a qualitative question about identity: the category of being Jesus occupies. Hoti kai hoi anemoi kai hē thalassa autō hypakouosin — that even the winds and the sea obey him — hypakouō (to obey, to hear under: to listen submissively): the same obedience-verb used for response to commands. The question the disciples ask ("what kind of person is this?") is the Christological question of the whole Gospel.
28: eis tēn chōran tōn Gadarēnōn — into the region of the Gadarenes — a Gentile region east of the Sea of Galilee; the textual tradition varies (Gadarenes, Gergesenes, Gerasenes) reflecting uncertainty about precise location, but all versions agree it is on the eastern (Gentile) shore. Dyo daimonizomenoi ek tōn mnēmeiōn — two demon-possessed men from the tombs — corpse-contamination made the tombs doubly unclean: the men live in the place of ritual impurity. Chalepoi lian — extremely violent (chalepos, harsh, dangerous, difficult): no one could pass.
29: ti hēmin kai soi — what to us and to you — a Semitic idiom of non-involvement or hostility (cf. John 2:4): "what do we have to do with you?" or "what business is there between us?", not a question seeking information but a declaration of contested relationship. Hyie tou theou — Son of God — the demons' identification of Jesus is accurate even where the disciples' understanding is incomplete (cf. 4:3,6, the devil's temptations use the same title). Pro kairou basanisai hēmas — to torment us before the time (kairos, the appointed time of eschatological judgment): the demons know of a future judgment and fear its premature arrival.
30: hē agelē tōn choirōn pollōn boskomenē — agelē — a large herd of pigs feeding (boskō, to feed by grazing, to tend; present passive participle) — pigs were unclean animals (Lev 11:7-8) and their presence confirms the Gentile context; Jews would not have kept swine. Makran ap autōn — some distance from them: the herd is within sight but separated.
31: hoi de daimones parekaloun auton legontes — the demons implored him saying (parakaleō, to beseech, the same word used of the centurion in v.5) — the demons ask rather than resist; their supplication acknowledges Jesus's authority to dispose of them as he chooses. Ei ekballeis hēmas aposteile hēmas eis tēn agelēn tōn choirōn — if you drive us out, send us into the herd of pigs — ei ekballeis (if you drive us out): the demons accept the inevitability of expulsion, requesting only the destination.
32: hypagete — go (hypago, aorist imperative: go away, depart) — a single word of command. Hoi de exelthontes apēlthon eis tous choirous — and departing they went into the pigs — the transition is immediate (aorist participles); no resistance is recorded. Kai idou hōrmēsen pasa hē agelē kata tou krēmnou eis tēn thalassan — and behold, the whole herd rushed down the cliff into the sea — hōrmēsen aorist of hormaō (to rush, surge forward, like a breaking wave): a single violent surge. Kai apethanon en tois hydasi — and died in the waters.
33: hoi de boskontes ephygon kai apelthontes eis tēn polin — the herdsmen fled and going into the city — ephygon aorist of pheugō (to flee): a flight of fear at what they witnessed. Apēngeilan panta kai ta tōn daimonizomenōn — they reported everything, including the things concerning the demon-possessed men — the two narrative threads (the pigs and the men) are both reported; the city learns of both.
34: kai idou pasa hē polis exēlthen eis hypantēsin tō Iēsou — and behold the whole city came out to meet Jesus — exēlthen aorist: a single collective movement out of the city toward him. Kai idontes auton parekalesan hopōs metabē apo tōn horiōn autōn — and seeing him they implored (parakaleō again) that he would depart from their region — the same parakaleō used by the centurion (v.5) and the demons (v.31) now used by the townspeople. The community chooses to lose Jesus rather than the economic disruption and social upheaval his presence brings. The irony: the demons beg to stay in the region; the people beg Jesus to leave it.
